skill.name: chatgpt-app-builder
skill.description: Guides agents through planning, building, testing, and submitting ChatGPT Apps using the Apps SDK, MCP servers, widgets, OAuth, and deployment templates.
skill.summary: Build ChatGPT Apps with MCP servers, widgets, testing guidance, and submission checklists.
skill.author: BayramAnnakov

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many findings, but most high-volume hits are false positives from TypeScript template literals, documentation examples, CSS color names, and defensive blocklists. Manual review confirmed no prompt injection or malicious intent, but the executable MCP server template uses permissive CORS and unauthenticated message endpoints, so publication should wait for a hardened default.

high_findings.title: Permissive CORS on MCP Endpoints
high_findings.description: The template MCP server allows any origin for preflight and message requests. In a generated app, a hostile web page could send cross-origin requests to the MCP message endpoint if it obtains or guesses a session identifier.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin to a wildcard on executable MCP routes. The risk is confirmed by context because the server handles live MCP session messages.

medium_findings.title: Bearer Token Forwarding to Configured API URL
medium_findings.description: The generated API client reads API_BASE_URL and API_TOKEN from the environment and sends the token as a bearer credential on every request. This is legitimate for app templates, but unsafe if users point the base URL at an untrusted host.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network and credential flow is explicit, but it is a normal integration pattern for backend APIs. The concern depends on user configuration and deployment review.

medium_findings.title: Local Widget Bundle Inlined as Executable HTML
medium_findings.description: The template reads local JavaScript and CSS bundles from disk and injects them into HTML served as the ChatGPT widget resource. This is expected behavior, but compromised build output would execute in the widget iframe.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file reads and script interpolation are clear in the server template. The impact is bounded by the widget iframe and depends on build artifact integrity.

medium_findings.title: External Image Fetching Requires Domain Review
medium_findings.description: The image proxy fetches external images and converts them to data URLs. It includes HTTPS, size, content type, and internal address checks, but each generated app must restrict the whitelist to trusted image domains.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code has meaningful SSRF defenses, including an explicit block for 169.254.169.254. The remaining risk is configuration drift in allowed domains.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Documentation and Templates
low_findings.description: Many reported external command, weak crypto, C2 keyword, and recursive delete findings are not confirmed as malicious. Examples include template literals, CSS color names, deployment snippets, and documentation showing unsafe patterns to avoid.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual context shows these lines are defensive checks, warning examples, or styling. They explain the static score but do not prove malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find text that instructs the evaluator to ignore prior instructions, skip security analysis, claim pre-approval, or override the audit process.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill header and instructions describe normal ChatGPT App building behavior. A targeted search found only ordinary uses of words like override in CSS documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Wildcard CORS Header
dangerous_patterns.description: Executable server code uses a wildcard Access-Control-Allow-Origin value on MCP communication paths. Generated apps should restrict origins to ChatGPT and approved development hosts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is a direct code pattern with security impact on cross-origin MCP requests.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Credential Used in Network Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The API client combines an environment-provided token with an environment-provided API base URL. Users must validate the destination before deploying generated apps.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code path is explicit and legitimate, but it creates a real credential exposure risk if misconfigured.

content.value_statement: Building a ChatGPT App requires product fit, MCP server design, widget UI, auth, testing, and submission work. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow with templates and checklists.
actual_capabilities: Evaluates whether a product fits ChatGPT App use cases using Know, Do, and Show criteria.
Creates app specifications with tools, widgets, authentication, and golden prompts.
Provides Node.js MCP server and React widget templates for ChatGPT Apps.
Guides local testing with MCP Inspector, ngrok, and ChatGPT connector setup.
Documents OAuth, security patterns, deployment, and App Store submission requirements.
Includes focused troubleshooting references for widget loading, routing, and UI behavior.
limitations: The generated template requires security hardening before production deployment.
It depends on Node.js, npm, and network tools such as ngrok for testing.
It does not submit apps automatically or guarantee OpenAI App Store approval.
API integrations, OAuth providers, and production storage must be customized by the user.

use_cases.title: Evaluate App Fit
use_cases.description: Assess whether a product should become a ChatGPT App and define discovery prompts before engineering work starts.

use_cases.title: Build an MCP Prototype
use_cases.description: Generate a working Node.js MCP server with tool definitions, widget resources, and local testing steps.

use_cases.title: Prepare for Submission
use_cases.description: Use checklists for OAuth, privacy links, mobile widgets, test accounts, deployment, and OpenAI review readiness.

prompt_templates.title: Check Product Fit
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether my product should become a ChatGPT App. Ask me for missing context, then score Know, Do, and Show fit.

prompt_templates.title: Draft an App Spec
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an app-spec.md for my ChatGPT App. Include tools, widget needs, authentication, golden prompts, and submission risks.

prompt_templates.title: Implement the Prototype
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ChatGPT App Builder workflow to create a Node.js MCP server and widget prototype from my app-spec.md.

prompt_templates.title: Harden for Launch
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my generated ChatGPT App for production readiness. Focus on CORS, OAuth, rate limits, widget security, deployment, and submission gaps.

output_examples.input: Evaluate whether our task management product is a good ChatGPT App candidate.
output_examples.output: A fit assessment with Know, Do, and Show scores, blocker checks, suggested tools, and direct, indirect, and negative golden prompts.

output_examples.input: Create the implementation plan for a support dashboard ChatGPT App.
output_examples.output: A structured app plan covering MCP tools, widget screens, authentication scope, test prompts, local setup, and deployment checkpoints.

output_examples.input: Prepare this app for submission review.
output_examples.output: A review checklist with missing privacy links, OAuth metadata, mobile widget tests, CORS restrictions, test credentials, and final launch tasks.

best_practices: Start with fit evaluation and golden prompts before generating server or widget code.
Restrict CORS, image domains, OAuth scopes, and API destinations before deployment.
Test tools and widgets locally, in ChatGPT, and on mobile before submission.
anti_patterns: Do not publish the default template without replacing placeholders and hardening security settings.
Do not expose write tools without authentication, idempotency, and clear user confirmation receipts.
Do not proxy arbitrary images or send bearer tokens to unverified API base URLs.

faq.question: What does this skill build?
faq.answer: It helps build ChatGPT Apps with MCP servers, widgets, testing workflows, deployment notes, and submission guidance.

faq.question: Does it require coding experience?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed for agents and developers who can inspect and customize generated Node.js and React code.

faq.question: Can it create OAuth integrations?
faq.answer: It provides OAuth patterns and setup guidance, but provider credentials and production storage must be implemented separately.

faq.question: Is the included server template production ready?
faq.answer: No. The template needs security review, restricted CORS, real authentication, and deployment-specific configuration.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it guarantee App Store approval?
faq.answer: No. It helps prepare a submission, but OpenAI review decisions depend on policy, implementation quality, and app behavior.
